No.

Reasoning:
- From the given probabilities, P(Zuph) = 0.34.
- P(¬Zuph ∧ Glimx) = 0.64 and P(Zuph ∧ Glimx) = 0.28.
- Therefore, P(Glimx) = P(¬Zuph ∧ Glimx) + P(Zuph ∧ Glimx) = 0.64 + 0.28 = 0.92.
- Conditional on Zuph: P(Glimx | Zuph) = P(Zuph ∧ Glimx) / P(Zuph) = 0.28 / 0.34 ≈ 0.8235.
- Conditional on ¬Zuph: P(Glimx | ¬Zuph) = P(¬Zuph ∧ Glimx) / P(¬Zuph) = 0.64 / 0.66 ≈ 0.9697.
- Since 0.8235 < 0.9697, the chance of Glimx is smaller when observing Zuph.

No. The probability of glimx is larger when observing zuph.

Reasoning:
- From the given numbers, P(zuph) = 0.34.
- P(not zuph and glimx) = 0.64, so P(¬zuph) = 1 − 0.34 = 0.66, which implies P(glimx | ¬zuph) = 0.64 / 0.66 ≈ 0.9697.
- P(zuph and glimx) = 0.28, so P(glimx | zuph) = 0.28 / 0.34 ≈ 0.8235.
- Therefore, P(glimx | zuph) ≈ 0.8235 is smaller than P(glimx | ¬zuph) ≈ 0.9697, but the question asks whether the chance of glimx is smaller when observing zuph (i.e., comparing P(glimx|zuph) to P(glimx) overall).
- Overall P(glimx) = P(zuph and glimx) + P(¬zuph and glimx) = 0.28 + 0.64 = 0.92.
- Since P(glimx | zuph) ≈ 0.8235 < 0.92, the chance of glimx is indeed smaller when observing zuph.

Thus, the correct answer is Yes, the chance of glimx is smaller when observing zuph.

No.

Reasoning:  
From the given probabilities:

- \(P(\text{zuph}) = 0.34\), so \(P(\text{not zuph}) = 0.66\).
- \(P(\text{not zuph} \cap \text{glimx}) = 0.64\).
- \(P(\text{zuph} \cap \text{glimx}) = 0.28\).

Compute the conditional probabilities of glimx:

- \(P(\text{glimx} \mid \text{not zuph}) = \frac{0.64}{0.66} \approx 0.9697\).
- \(P(\text{glimx} \mid \text{zuph}) = \frac{0.28}{0.34} \approx 0.8235\).

Since \(0.8235 < 0.9697\), the chance of glimx is indeed smaller when observing zuph. This reflects that, in this specific numerical setup, glimx is more likely when zuph is absent than when it is present, even though zuph has a direct causal effect on glimx in the described causal graph.

No. In this hypothetical world, glimx is a common effect (a collider) of zory and zuph. Conditioning on the cause (zuph) does not reduce the probability of the effect (glimx) in the way a common cause would; rather, it can increase it. Here, the probability of glimx given zuph is 28% / 34% ≈ 82.4%, while the probability of glimx given not zuph is (64% / 66%) ≈ 97%. Since 82.4% < 97%, the chance of glimx is indeed smaller when observing zuph. This counterintuitive result arises because zory is the other parent of glimx, and without information about zory, the absence of zuph (not zuph) allows zory to more freely influence glimx, increasing its overall probability in that subgroup. Thus, observing zuph reduces the conditional probability of glimx compared to not observing zuph.
